sentences.text: Ready?
I did.
Yes, my father was taken away.
He got an order to report for forced labor.
The Hungarian army, in 1942.
And he was taken from us for several months.
But before he left-- and, and the forced labor went with the, with the army itself, and they were working on the Soviet front or Russian front.

And before he went, he contacted a friend of his, a medical doctor, and asked for certain pills to be given to him, which would make his heart go-- well, not bad, but which would cause heart troubles to him so, so they would, they would release him, because of a heart condition.
And fortunately, my mom prayed for this.
And finally, he showed up at home.
But I think it was no-- without consequence.
In other words, he really did damage his heart.

He-- when he came back in 1942 until 1944, he was not the same man as we have known him, because of this heart problem, which he pretty much took upon himself by taking these medications, which he probably should not have taken.
And the only reason why he took them-- so he can be home and released from, from this forced labor unit.
These forced labor units were called [NON-ENGLISH], which is a whole, long name.
But basically, what they were is they were working with the, the armed units.
And they had to dig tunnels and bridges and work on airports.
But their equipment was only shovel, so this was-- because they didn't trust the Jews.
Otherwise-- and I didn't mention it, that my father was a decorated person from World War I.
He served, I think, two years, in World War I, and-- but this was all disregarded in 1942.

That didn't matter anymore, whether you were an officer or you were-- you know, you served, you, you got some kind of a, a wound in a-- just like in case of my, my uncle, for instance, who was, I think, a captain or major in World War I.
He had a shrapnel to his, his face, his mouth.
And there was no excuse for that.
They, they couldn't take him for, for forced labor, for [NON-ENGLISH].
But they took him in the middle of the night, like a political--
No, my uncle in the middle of the night, like a political prisoner.
Yes.
Yes, were many, many people were taken.
And many people got orders to report in a city called Debrecen, D-E-B-R-E-C-E-N, where they had to report.
And this was the day when we had to report ourselves, to the ghetto.
And this was the last time we, we saw him.
Well, I can't say we heard of him.
Because he, he sent a, a postcard once.
But that was after we came back we saw the postcard.
Yes.
Yes.
At that time, at that time, after March 19, things got accelerated.
We had to wear the, the Star of David.
And we had to vacate our home.
And then we moved in with my, my mother's uncle and aunt.
And then, where we were-- while we were over there, we got the orders to, to report to the ghetto.
And they took our belongings.
They took our jewelry.
They took our-- everything what we had, practically.
And we just had a small supply what we were able to bring into the ghetto.
I think it was, like, 24 hours or 48 hours, the most.
And by the way, when we went to the ghetto-- and that just shows you how the, the Nazi propaganda afflicted, or affected, the people.
People started laughing.
And people started cheering.
And of course, I'm talking about the general Hungarian population.
And it was a sad sight.
And I-- we were bleeding from the inside because of that.
Because those were people who perhaps were our-- well, not friends, but our clients and neighbors.

And these were the people who-- and they were hardly waiting for, for the Jews to, to leave their homes so they can rob them, so they can go to their homes and rob them from their possessions and take everything away from them.
Because they figured these people will never come back, anyway.
My recollection isn't too clear on that.
I, I, I don't even know what I took.
If anything, probably just a pair of slacks or shoes and, you know, coat.
No, I, I don't think so.
I don't think so.
When we checked into the ghetto, there were gendarmes.
And the gendarmes were worse.
This was not police.
The gendarme was like more of a military-type operation.
And this was other than Budapest, like in the suburbs.
And gendarmes, which we called [HUNGARIAN ]-- these were very vicious people, very vicious people.
Hungarian.
These were Hungarian gendarmes.
And they were the ones who controlled the ghetto.
And of course, the, the SS controlled it as well.
Now in the ghetto, they formed a, like a Jewish committee of the elders.
And my father's first cousin, another lawyer, was a member-- or I'm not sure-- perhaps president of that Jewish committee.
His name was Dr.
Csato, C-S-A-T-O, David.
And he was a first cousin of my husband.
He was much older than my father was.
And he was also a very respected lawyer in, in Szolnok.
And he was one of the main members of this committee.
But the committee was actually powerless.
And talking about the ghetto, I wanted to bring up this little tale about my mother's uncle and aunt.
My uncle was a converted Jew.
And he had papers, which he, he showed at the ghetto, where he had to report to, that he was converted.
And they said, it doesn't matter.
You have to come into the ghetto anyway.
And then, what happened was that his wife, who was German and Roman Catholic, went and she was pleading with the commandant of the ghetto, who was German.
And in German, she begged him, let my husband get out of the ghetto.
And he said, no way.
The only way-- she said, we want to be together.
She-- he said to her, the only way you can be together with, with him, if you yourself come into the ghetto.
And she refused to do so.
So this is how they got separated.
And of course, the sad story of it is that Oscar, who was my, my mother's aunt-- he was killed.
My, my mother's uncle.
He was, he was killed.
And we don't know the exact circumstances how.
And he was a decorated-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I want to say major.
But he was a higher up than a major-- a colonel.
He was a, he was a colonel from World War I who lived on pension.
Well, the ghetto was very large.
All the, the Jews from Szolnok were in the ghetto.
The ghetto consisted of, basically, the, the former Jewish elementary school where I used to go to and the neighboring houses.
It was like city blocks long.
And within the ghetto, from one house to another, of course, you had access.
But you couldn't go outside of the ghetto at all.
And in a ghetto, of course, you just had what was there in, in the houses, you know.
If there were beds there, you know, you used it.
But there were many, many people.
The Jews of Szolnok were a large number.
And, and this large number of Jews was all contained in this, in this ghetto area.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
I don't remember exactly.
As I said, they, they were like, like houses, you know, like houses.
And I think they were like houses of four or five rooms.
And then each house contained x many people.
But I don't remember exactly, you know.
It varied from one house to another and from one room to another.
But pretty much you, you had, I think, a selection.
You could go to another house or-- but it, it wouldn't matter, because space was scarce.
No matter where you went, it was very scarce.
And the main thing was that you wanted to stay together.
We, for instance, stayed together with the Csatos-- Dr.
Csato-- David-- his wife, Esther, and his daughter, Ava.
And we were together like one family because we were very close to each other.
And this continued to the later years, as well, as, as I will talk about it later on.
Food was available in the ghetto, but it was rationed.
It was rationed.
So whatever it was-- and then some people were able to, I think, get some food from the outside.
I don't know exactly how they did it.
But, but there was food available.
But the main thing was that the ghetto was just a, a transition point from worse things to come.
So even if it was food available and were somewhat sanitary facilities available, that was just a transitory period, what we had in the ghetto.
No, but the entrances were strictly controlled.
They, they put up wooded gates.
Everything was, was gated.
And there was a wooded gate around it.
And it was-- constantly, it was patrolled by the, by the gendarmes and the local police, too.
But mainly the gendarmes we had to, we had to watch.
And of course, the SS was there.
So, so you didn't try to, to escape from the ghetto.
There was no escaping from there.
Well, they were trying to maintain order.
Some people became real depressed and suicidal.
And some people tried to take advantage, perhaps, of another person.
They tried to-- well, the word is probably finagle a little more space or more food or something.
And of course, it was the Jewish committee's responsibility to crack down on those abuses.
Mainly, they were just an instrument of the SS.
That's, that's all they were, an instrument of the SS to carry out whatever the SS said that they wanted the people to do.

If they wanted the people to stand in line and get counted three times a day, the Jewish committee was responsible for that, to make sure that everybody knows that at certain time, at this time, there will be a, a listing or the numbering of the names.
And you have to stand in line, you know, strict, erect, and don't do anything else at that particular time.
I was exactly 14.
Every day of the ghetto, some people were praying.
Some people were talking and politicking.
And to be honest with you, I don't remember.
I probably prayed with some of the other people.
We prayed that there, there would be some kind of a solution.
Perhaps Regent Horthy or somebody from America will come up with a solution and, and effect the Germans to let us go, let us go back to our homes.
No, this was strictly Szolnok.
That was strictly Szolnok.
The, the larger camp, which we went subsequent to this, was in the outskirts of Szolnok.
And that was at the, the sugar factory in Szolnok, which was really made up as a concentration camp with barbed, electric barbed wires.
And that was a place which was holding not only the city of Szolnok, but all the surrounding areas-- all the Jews from the surrounding areas.
I don't know how far from it.
But that was an absolute nightmare.
That was a horror compared to the ghetto.
We were taken to the sugar factory.
I never forget it, that we, we went out with the rabbi of the congregation, Dr.
Heves [INAUDIBLE] and his wife.
And people were laughing and having a heck of a good time in the, in the streets when they saw us going to the, to the ghetto.
No, we did not walk.
We, we were able to go on, on carriages, horse-drawn carriage.
The ghetto was just probably-- oh, probably two, three weeks.
No, nothing.
We haven't heard anything from the outside world.
No, we never did.
We never did.
We never even heard the word Auschwitz when we were in the sugar factory, which in Hungarian is called [HUNGARIAN]-- [HUNGARIAN] for sugar, [HUNGARIAN] for factory.
And this-- we found out that the reason why they put us to the sugar factory, because there were railroad tracks leading into and out of the sugar factory.
And this area-- we were just staying in, like, storage facilities, like, you know, cement floors.
There was absolutely nothing there, you know.
You, you couldn't even lie down.
There was so little room.
Now, what we had to do is dug [HUNGARIAN], which is like outhouses.
But it's not outhouses.
It was like everybody had to do, do it in front of other people, which was absolutely devastating.
And there was no food.
There was very little water to drink.
And people got so desperate, they went against the, the barbed wire.
And of course, they were killed from the barbed wire.
And lots of times, we heard shots at night because-- and during the daytime, too-- because people got so depressed and desperate.
For any reason, they, they wanted just to get away.
Of course, they couldn't.
Now, the other thing is the fear.
This was controlled not by the gendarmes, anymore.
This was controlled by the SS.
And they walked around all day long and all night long with those German Shepherd dogs.
And those German Shepherd dogs were trained to kill.
You couldn't fool around with them.
And every time, you had to stand in line.
And we were wasted away, you know, from the facilities.
There was no facilities for, for cleaning clean, no facilities to eat.
And we didn't know exactly what, what's going to happen.
Now, at this point in time, it was very interesting that the Jewish committee told people that there would be two transports going out of sugar factory.
The first transport would contain people whose father or brother or son is in the forced labor, which is, I said, [HUNGARIAN].
Now, in the second transport-- people who have no immediate relatives in, in forced labor.
Now, my mother wanted to stay together with her sister-in-law, which is [NON-ENGLISH], Solti [NON-ENGLISH], who was my uncle-- his wife, Rosza, [NON-ENGLISH], and her two daughters.
She wanted to stay together with them.
But [NON-ENGLISH] was not in forced labor.
So in other words, they had to go with the second transport.
We are supposed to be going with the first transport.
And my mother wanted to change.
We didn't know where those transports are going or where they are going to take us.
We had absolutely no idea about Auschwitz or anything.
And I begged with my mother.
I said, look, this is, this is the reasoning.
This is the logic you have to follow here.
You can go from the first transport to the second.
But you cannot go from the second to the first.
So the first transport must be somewhat better than the second.
And of course, we didn't know what we are talking about.
We are just kind of guessing.
So to make a long story short, we stayed with the first transport.
And we went with our relatives, Dr.
Csato and his family.
And the first transport landed in Austria.
Second transport-- Auschwitz.
So very few people came back from, from the second transport.
And as far as how, how many people, I don't know.
But as I said, all Szolnok and all the surrounding vicinities, the little towns and villages-- all those people were in the concentration camp.
Yes.
This was the first transport.
We went day and night in the box cars, of course.
The train was a long train.
We had no-- we couldn't see out.
We had no idea where we are going.
Some people were trying to guess at perhaps we are going towards Austria or perhaps we are going past Vienna-- [NON-ENGLISH].
But we had absolutely no idea.
And it was like, I would say, probably three or four days.
Because these railroad trains were kept off the normal routine, where the regular passenger trains are going.
They were going not by the, by the stations, but they were going, like, the 10th or 15th track from the stations so people would not hear us, you know, see us.
And of course, I don't know if people really knew what was going on or not at that particular time and moment.
But finally, we arrived to Austria, city of Strasshof.
Yes.
Well, two things-- well, first of all, the fear.
And secondly, I was just hoping that I can stay together with my mother.
These were the only two considerations.
Because I knew that if we stay together, we can help each other.
And she can comfort me.
Because I was in a-- physically, I was in a very emaciated stage.
I was very weak.
I had dysentery, from probably drinking something which wasn't probably even water, or somehow some infectious bacteria got into, into my stomach, my intestines.
And I could hardly stand up or could hardly, could hardly walk.
And my mother was the inspiration behind me.
She said, you must walk.
You must walk.
Because if you don't walk, then they will shot you to death.
So she was my inspiration in that.
And, and the only thing what I had was fear and the inspiration for-- coming from her to, to get going.
All these things get kind of blurry.
[LAUGHS] After 50 years, these things are kind of blurry.
I know it was a what they called in German [GERMAN], which was a concentration camp for transit.
And there were thousands and thousands of people there.
And they all arrived from, from various wagons and from various parts of the countries, not only from Hungary but from other parts of other countries as well.
Of course, mind you, that Hungary was the last country which actually was taken by Hitler.
And we are the last ones who got, got being exterminated.
But nevertheless, when we arrived, we had, again, no idea where we are and what the situation will be here, what the circumstances will bring.
We just hope that we can survive from one day to the next.
And that was, that was also a crucial thing, especially in my physical state and in my physical condition.
My mother-- thank God.
She was fairly strong.
And as I said before, she knew the, the German language perfectly.
She sounded like a real, true German, with her, her [GERMAN] accent, with high German accent.
And that, that served for our advantage all the, all the years, too.
But Strasshof we managed-- people from Szolnok managed together, together.
And finally, when the call came that they needed people for work in, in Austria, then 21 people said that they, they want to go together.
And these were the people from Szolnok.
Strasshof?
Probably just one week.
The whole thing was one week.
One long, long week.
Well, I think everybody, everybody tried to, to follow somebody.
Like, most people really looked up to Dr.
Csato, our friend and cousin, the lawyer.
And they, they took him as, as kind of a, a leader, perhaps a spiritual leader.
Or they figured he might bring some good luck to, to the rest of us.
And we all got together with him.
There were, like, three younger children-- me, myself, and a brother and sister.
And the rest of them were from various walks of life.
There was a lady whose husband was another lawyer.
There was a house painter.
There was an insurance agent.
Various people got together from Szolnok, who knew each other, by the way, from [INAUDIBLE], or just they knew each other, period, you know.
And, and we, we decided to stay together.
And when the call came that they needed these people, we volunteered.
And we went to a place, which turned out to be a, an Austrian duchess'-- actually, her estate, forestry, and the land estate.
And we were selected to, to work on this estate.
Yes.
The name was [GERMAN], which means-- [GERMAN] means forestry.
And Auersperg was their family name.
They were, apparently, aristocrats, you know, related somewhat to Franz Joseph, the emperor of Austria-Hungary.
And they lived in two lavish mansions, about 100 rooms each.
But it was all controlled by the SS, you know.
I think their estate was taken away by the SS.
And they really had no say so in what's, what's happening.
I have a strange suspicion that they kind of felt sorry for us, for the Jews.
But they had no say in it.
They couldn't show it.
Because even their own servants-- they, they were members of the Nazi party.
For instance, one of our people, who supervised us, his title was [GERMAN], which means chief hunter.
And he walked around with his, with his gun on his shoulders.
And, and he was, he was a Nazi.
He was a devout Nazi as, as you can get.
And of course, there was the SS person there.
And he walked around.
And he watched everything.
And he watched everybody.
And he perhaps watched even the, the duchess and, and the count.
One of the, the ladies, the duchess' daughter, was married to a count.
And they lived in another place.
And the only connection we had with them that they saw us, and we saw them.
And once, or a few times, me and a friend of mine were invited to bring woods into their kitchen.
And then we got a little extra treat, you know, a little milk or something, a little bread.
But we lived in a deep forest.
We lived in a little-- well, actually the place where it was was at the border of Austria and Czechoslovakia, which used to be Czechoslovakia.
And there was a river there named Thaya River.
And where they put us was a little border post, border, a guard post, by the river on the Austrian side.
And we were told never try to, to escape by swimming over the river.
Because we would perish.
We would be shot.
Because they were watching the river.
Now, of course, both sides belonged to Hitler.
But still, they were watching the river.
So-- but where we lived, there was nobody around us.
Only 21 of us lived there.
And there was one man who was the-- our commandant, who was the head of, head of us.
And he was an orthodox Jew from Szolnok, of course.
And his name was--
Yes, his name was Samuel, S-A-M-U-E-L.
His first name was Miksa, M-I-K-S-A.
And he was there with his wife and his daughter.
His daughter was about 18, 19 at that time.
Her name is [NON-ENGLISH], Susan.
And he, he was entrusted by the SS to, to watch us.
And of course, we were trying, we were trying not to escape.
We are trying to behave.
Every day, we walked up the, the hills.
We were walking through the woods, whether it was snow or rain or sleet and started taking, cutting the huge trees out.
And once those trees were cut, we had to chop them up for, for firewood.
